saida_bruta:
There was nothing gigantic about the man who now stirred toward waking on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial house in that residential district of Zenith known as Floral Heights.

His name was George F. Babbitt. He was forty-six years old this April of 1920, and he produced nothing tangible—no butter, no shoes, no poetry—but he was deft in the art of selling houses for more than people could afford.

His broad head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. His face looked childlike in sleep, despite its wrinkles and the red dents his spectacles had pressed into his nose. He was not fat, exactly, but very well fed; his cheeks were soft as cushions, and the smooth, idle hand resting on the khaki blanket was faintly puffy. He looked prosperous, thoroughly married, and utterly unromantic—as unromantic as the sleeping-porch itself, which overlooked one sizable elm, two tidy grass-plots, a cement driveway, and a corrugated iron garage. And yet Babbitt was dreaming once more of the fairy child—a dream more romantic than any scarlet pagoda beside a silver sea.
saida_filtrada:
There was nothing gigantic about the man who now stirred toward waking on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial house in that residential district of Zenith known as Floral Heights.

His name was George F. Babbitt. He was forty-six years old this April of 1920, and he produced nothing tangible—no butter, no shoes, no poetry—but he was deft in the art of selling houses for more than people could afford.

His broad head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. His face looked childlike in sleep, despite its wrinkles and the red dents his spectacles had pressed into his nose. He was not fat, exactly, but very well fed; his cheeks were soft as cushions, and the smooth, idle hand resting on the khaki blanket was faintly puffy. He looked prosperous, thoroughly married, and utterly unromantic—as unromantic as the sleeping-porch itself, which overlooked one sizable elm, two tidy grass-plots, a cement driveway, and a corrugated iron garage. And yet Babbitt was dreaming once more of the fairy child—a dream more romantic than any scarlet pagoda beside a silver sea.

saida_bruta: For years the fairy child had come to him in dreams. Where others saw only a portly, unremarkable man of business, she alone perceived his true self: gallant and brave. Real life could not touch her—she waited for him beyond the rim of the actual, in cool dim groves. She would run to him, slim and eager, and her hand would find his, and together they would fly toward the stars, laughing, until the roofs of the sleeping city fell far behind and it seemed the whole gray, careful world had never existed at all.

She would run to him, slim and eager, and her hand would find his, and together they would fly toward the stars, laughing, until the roofs of the sleeping city fell far behind and it seemed the whole gray, careful world had never existed at all.
